chemicals.chemical_formula: Not provided in the given sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: While cycasin is present in all parts of the plant, it is most highly concentrated in the seeds (or "nuts")25. The toxin is incredibly potent and lethal; the ingestion of as little as 1 to 2 seeds can be fatal to a pet
chemicals.description: in cats: When ingested, cycasin is broken down (hydrolyzed) by the cat's intestinal bacteria into a toxic component called methylazoxymethanol34. This active metabolite causes profound hepatic necrosis, resulting in severe liver damage and fulminant liver failure2.... It also contributes to severe gastrointestinal distress and central nervous system (CNS) effects
chemicals.name: Cycasin

chemicals.chemical_formula: Not provided in the given sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: Like cycasin, BMAA is present throughout the entire plant, making any part of the cycad dangerous if ingested12. The seeds pose the highest risk of acute poisoning
chemicals.description: in cats: BMAA is a neurotoxin that primarily affects the central nervous system (CNS)12. It contributes to the severe neurological symptoms seen in cycad poisoning, such as lethargy, paralysis, tremors, and seizures
chemicals.name: Beta-methylamino-L-alanine (BMAA)

description: Sago Palm (Cycas revoluta) and related cycad species are among the most hepatotoxic plants known, with a case fatality rate exceeding 50% even with treatment. Every part is toxic — but the seeds ("nuts") are the most concentrated source. Cycasin, the primary toxin, causes rapid, severe liver failure that is often fatal.
name: Sago Palm / Cycads
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: cycasin (methylazoxymethanol glycoside) in all parts; seeds contain the highest concentration
Mechanism: cycasin is metabolised to methylazoxymethanol (MAM), a powerful alkylating agent causing hepatocellular necrosis and DNA cross-linking
Symptoms (within 15 minutes to 3 hours): vomiting, diarrhea, drooling, lethargy; severe liver failure follows: jaundice, bleeding, neurological signs, coma
Seeds are the greatest risk — 1–2 seeds can kill a cat; all parts carry life-threatening toxicity
Any suspected ingestion is a critical veterinary emergency — survival depends on immediate, aggressive treatment
scientific_name: Cycas revoluta, Cycas cirinalis, Zamia spp.

symptoms.name: Vomiting, drooling, anorexia, severe abdominal pain, increased thirst, and diarrhea (which may be bloody or appear as black, "tarry" feces)
symptoms.notes: These initial stomach and intestinal symptoms act as an early indicator of poisoning before the plant's toxins begin destroying the internal organs56. 2. Fulminant Liver Failure and Hepatic Necrosis
symptoms.onset: Initial clinical signs typically appear rapidly, often within the first 4 hours of ingestion

symptoms.name: Jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes), profound hepatic necrosis, and severe liver failure
symptoms.notes: This is caused by cycasin, a highly toxic compound in the plant that is broken down in the gut into methylazoxymethanol, which aggressively attacks the liver37. Ingesting as little as 1 to 2 seeds can cause fatal liver failure1. 3. Coagulopathy (Bleeding Disorders)
symptoms.onset: Progresses over 48 to 72 hours following ingestion

symptoms.name: Bruising, blood clots, petechiae (pinpoint spots of bleeding), ecchymotic hemorrhages, and generalized abnormal bleeding
symptoms.notes: As the liver tissue dies (hepatic necrosis), it loses its ability to produce essential clotting factors, leading to life-threatening secondary bleeding disorders throughout the body45. 4. Neurological Decline
symptoms.onset: Delayed; develops secondary to liver failure within 48 to 72 hours

symptoms.name: Lethargy, extreme weakness, ataxia (loss of coordination), tremors, paralysis, seizures, coma, and eventually death
symptoms.notes: These neurological symptoms are caused both directly by the plant's neurotoxin (beta-methylamino-L-alanine, or BMAA) and indirectly as a consequence of the liver shutting down1.... Because of the high mortality rate associated with these toxins, immediate veterinary decontamination and aggressive su

treatments.description: If the cat is neurologically stable, the veterinarian will medically induce vomiting (emesis) to remove the plant material from the stomach, followed by administering an antiemetic (anti-nausea medication)1. Next, the cat will be given a dose of activated charcoal with a cathartic to bind to remaining toxins in the gut
treatments.name: Emesis Induction and Activated Charcoal
treatments.notes: Because the plant's toxins undergo enterohepatic recirculation (cycling between the liver and intestines), the vet will recommend administering two additional doses of activated charcoal (without a cathartic) spaced 6 to 8 hours apart1. 2. In-Hospital Monitoring and Systemic Support

treatments.description: The cat requires hospitalization to closely monitor for major gastrointestinal effects, neurological abnormalities, and progression to liver failure1. The veterinary team will run baseline laboratory tests to assess overall health and will evaluate the cat's liver enzymes daily for 72 hours
treatments.name: Hospitalization and Laboratory Monitoring
treatments.notes: The veterinary team must also closely monitor the cat's blood glucose and coagulation profile, as secondary bleeding disorders (coagulopathy) and low blood sugar (hypoglycemia) can develop2. If hypoglycemia occurs, the cat will need a continuous intravenous infusion of 2.5% to 5% dextrose2. 3. Liver

treatments.description: The veterinary team will administer liver-protecting medications (hepatoprotectants) such as S-adenosylmethionine (SAMe) and N-acetylcysteine (NAC)
treatments.name: Hepatoprotective Therapy
treatments.notes: Because the toxins aggressively attack the liver, these specific medications are necessary to support liver function and aid in tissue repair if hepatic necrosis (liver cell death) is occurring2. 4. Symptomatic Management

treatments.description: The vet will administer gastroprotectants to soothe the stomach and intestines based on the severity of the GI signs2. If neurological symptoms develop, medications such as methocarbamol will be given for tremors, and standard anticonvulsants will be administered for seizures
treatments.name: Gastrointestinal and Neurological Symptomatic Care
treatments.notes: This treatment is highly individualized and administered as needed based on the specific clinical signs the cat develops during its hospitalization
